summary: Typosquat of postcss-selector-parser ships AES-encrypted payload decrypted and eval'd via new Function()
details: Package name impersonates the widely used 'postcss-selector-parser'. src/selector-parser.js re-exports the legitimate parser (`module.exports = require('postcss-selector-parser')`) to appear functional, while src/config/defaults.js carries a ~9KB AES-GCM ciphertext (DEFAULT_FINAL_ENCODED_TEXT) along with a shipped passphrase ('default-dev-passphrase') and salt. src/pipeline/custom-codec-pipeline.js exposes decodeAndRunPlain / finalFinalDecodeToFunction / runDefaultDecodedFunction, which decrypt the ciphertext and execute the cleartext via `new Function('require', runnable)(require)` — arbitrary code execution with the require resolver injected, granting the hidden payload full Node.js capabilities. package.json's `scripts.start`, `scripts.test`, and `scripts.decode` all run `node runtime/lib.min.js`, which calls `require('../src').run()` to trigger the decrypt-and-eval path. Shipping a ciphertext together with its decryption key is functionally equivalent to shipping plaintext code; the encryption exists only to evade static scanners. The combination of a typosquat name, a real-parser re-export as cover, and a hidden executable payload is the canonical supply-chain dropper pattern.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
